lesson.title: Conditional Probability & Bayes
lesson.body: The conditional probability of A given B is P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B), defined when P(B) > 0. The multiplication rule P(A∩B) = P(A|B)·P(B) extends to chains of events. The law of total probability states P(A) = Σᵢ P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ) for any partition {Bᵢ} of Ω. Bayes' theorem inverts conditional probabilities: P(B|A) = P(A|B)P(B) / P(A), which is the foundation of Bayesian inference.

rooms.question: P(A ∩ B) = 0.12 and P(B) = 0.4. What is P(A | B)?
steps: Substitute the given values into the definition.

rooms.question: A card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. Given that the card is red, what is the probability it is a heart?
steps: Hearts are red, so Heart \cap Red = Heart. There are 13 hearts.
rooms.explanation: All hearts are red, so P(Heart ∩ Red) = 13/52. P(Red) = 26/52. Thus P(Heart|Red) = (13/52)/(26/52) = 13/26 = 1/2.

rooms.question: A disease affects 1% of the population. A test is 95% sensitive (P(+|disease) = 0.95) and 90% specific (P(−|no disease) = 0.90). Using Bayes' theorem, what is P(disease | +)?
rooms.explanation: P(D|+) = (0.95×0.01) / (0.95×0.01 + 0.10×0.99) = 0.0095/0.1085 ≈ 0.0876. Despite a 95% sensitivity, the low prevalence means most positives are false alarms.

rooms.question: Events A and B are independent. Which equation must hold?
steps: Independence means knowing B gives no information about A.
So P(A|B) = P(A), and the multiplication rule simplifies.
rooms.explanation: By definition, A and B are independent if and only if P(A ∩ B) = P(A)·P(B). This is equivalent to P(A|B) = P(A) whenever P(B) > 0.

rooms.question: Order the steps to apply Bayes' theorem correctly when computing P(B₁ | A) given a partition {B₁, B₂, B₃}:
items: Compute P(A) = \sum_i P(A|B_i)P(B_i)  [law of total probability]
Identify prior probabilities P(B_i) and likelihoods P(A|B_i)
rooms.explanation: Step 1: gather the priors and likelihoods. Step 2: use total probability to find P(A). Step 3: compute the numerator. Step 4: divide to get the posterior P(B₁|A).

boss.name: The Reverend Bayes
boss.taunt: Your prior is worthless and your likelihood is fiction. Update your beliefs — or perish!

questions.question: A bag contains 3 red and 2 blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. Given that the first ball is red, what is P(second ball is blue)?
questions.explanation: After drawing one red ball, 4 balls remain: 2 red and 2 blue. So P(second is blue | first is red) = 2/4 = 1/2.

questions.question: P(A) = 0.5, P(B|A) = 0.6, P(B|Aᶜ) = 0.2. Compute P(A|B) using Bayes' theorem.
Factory A produces 60% of widgets; factory B produces 40%. The defect rate is 2% from A and 5% from B. A randomly chosen widget is defective. What is the probability it came from factory B?
questions.explanation: P(D) = 0.02×0.6 + 0.05×0.4 = 0.012 + 0.020 = 0.032. P(B|D) = (0.05×0.4)/0.032 = 0.020/0.032 = 0.625. Despite B being the minority producer, its higher defect rate means a defective widget is more likely to have come from B.
